11 min read + 21 min video
▪ “Your newsletter is like a pot of gold delivered every week…”
▪ “Easily one of the most valuable emails I get every week…”
▪ “This is my favorite newsletter. I have told everyone about it…”
▪ “Seriously one of the most actionable things I’ve read all year…”
▪ “It’s making me smarter…”
These are the sort of comments you want your audience to say or
think about your Value Newsletter. Let’s talk about how to make
this happen.
Value Newsletter (VNL)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 14 --

We’re living through a digital newsletter Renaissance right now,
and we don’t see this trend slowing down any time soon.
The humble “newsletter” now has teeth, and professional
newsletter publishers are sprouting up like mushrooms,
including little one-person indie operations, like Lenny Rachitsky
who has over 70K subscribers, and earns 6-�gures a year from his
indie newsletter.
Morning Brew was one of the �rst movers and shakers that got
the attention of Silicon Valley. They quickly grew an audience (of
millions!) on the back of a single email newsletter done well.
(Morning Brew was acquired by Business Insider for $75M on Oct
30, 2020. That’s retiring money.)
Hell, even Arnold Schwarzenegger has a newsletter, and it’s a
damn good one; it reads like a memoir. In fact, it’s one of the best
personal-brand newsletters we’ve read. Arnold writes the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 14 --

newsletter himself, and signs off with his impressive signature.
For us, however, we think about the role of the humble newsletter
(or the toothy one), a little differently.
A newsletter is not “the thing” we do, but rather part of a strategic
whole, contributing to the performance of a larger email system.
Before any subscriber can receive a newsletter broadcast (or
evergreen newsletter), they have to go through a Relationship
Building Series (RBS); this is non-negotiable.
This lays the foundation on which we can continue to work to
build trust and earn attention over time (Trust Engine).
We’re presenting this Module in two sections — in the �rst
section, we’ll unpack how we think about a Value Newsletter.
Then in section two, we’ll link to other newsletters to deepen your
perspective of what’s possible.
The Value Newsletter
A Value Newsletter is a vehicle for delivering value to an audience
over time, deepening relationships and building trust along the
way, while creating opportunities to demonstrate empathy,
authority, credibility, and preeminence.
Value Newsletters also create additional layers and connections in
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 14 --

the world you’re building for your audience.
‘Value’ is subjective and unique to your relationship with your
audience and their speci�c needs and desires as those needs and
desires relate to your experience and areas of expertise. Therefore,
we can’t de�ne value for you. So instead, we’ll share ideas and
examples you can use to inspire your thinking.
It’s also important to know that creating value affects you — the
creator — as well as your audience.
For you, a Value Newsletter can be a creative outlet that inspires
and captures your thinking about subjects that matter to you (and
your audience).
A Value Newsletter also can be a mechanism to engage with your
audience explicitly and implicitly to guide your ideas in the
future.
For example, in early 2021 we used our Emergent Marketing
Newsletter to ask our audience eight speci�c questions and one
open-ended question, and we received hundreds of replies.
That explicit interaction informed a lot of the content we created
in 2021, guided the development of the Momentum Builder
Workshop (Lean Edition), and helped us focus our energy and
attention on topics we knew — in advance — would resonate with
our audience.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 14 --

We also receive feedback every time we send an Emergent
Marketing newsletter. Occasionally we ask for feedback about
speci�c questions (explicitly), other times we get feedback based
on individual reactions (implicitly).
There’s no formula to determine when to ask your audience for
feedback directly (through questions, surveys, etc.), and nothing
says you have to do that. (We think it’s useful, for us, and we
suspect it’s useful for the majority of people reading this module
as well.)
Committing to creating a Value Newsletter can be used as a
forcing mechanism as well. With all of the demands of a business,
it’s very easy to get distracted and forget to engage with your
audience regularly.
A consistent (or semi-consistent) VNL is an effective reminder to
maintain, expand, and enhance your connection with your
audience over a long time horizon.
A VNL can be a placeholder for one of the most important
activities we do, which is developing and enhancing our
relationship with our audience.
We’ve all been on email lists where the creator disappears until
there’s something to sell. Rather than have to make excuses for
that mistake, we recommend eliminating the possibility of
making that mistake entirely by committing to a Value
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 14 --

Newsletter.
(One way to think about a VNL is as an upstream decision that
eliminates many potential downstream problems…)
Once you accept and internalize the importance of a VNL as part
of your email marketing system, the next question is what,
exactly, should you include in your Value Newsletter?
The opportunities are endless.
“What is most valuable to my audience?” That is where everything
begins.
“What frequency can I sustain that is most appropriate for the
needs and desires of my audience?”
The �rst part of that question — “what frequency can I sustain?”
— is critically important. If a weekly email would be ideal for your
audience, and soul-crushing for you, don’t commit to a weekly
VNL!
Do less, better is an effective guiding principle in this example
(and in general). If you think you can create a VNL every two
weeks consistently, try every three weeks �rst (or every month).
Create the rules of the game so you can win every time, and only
adapt those rules if you know you can continue to win.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 14 --

You can always change the frequency of your emails (we have
several times). Frequency is less important than consistency — tell
your audience what to expect, then deliver on those expectations.
It’s as simple as that.
“What type of value will I share with my audience?” Broadly
speaking, there are three options to consider:
▪ Creating content yourself.
▪ Curating content created by others.
▪ A hybrid that combines creating and curating.
In general, creating content yourself is more time-intensive than
assembling (and perhaps commenting on) content created by
others.
Again, the best way to answer this question is to determine what
is most useful to your audience.
We take a hybrid approach where 90% or more of the content we
share is self-generated. We also often link to outside resources
(ours and others), and occasionally we create ‘best of’
compilations that are curated lists.
Here are a few examples for reference.
Our 2020 in Review email is an extensive curated list of content
we created in 2020. The P.S. in that email is André’s list of book
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 14 --

recommendations, and the P.P.S. is Shawn’s list of podcast
recommendations.
Lists within lists within lists…
A Simple Testing Framework is an example of content we created,
�rst as an Emergent Marketing Newsletter which we later
repurposed as content on the TLB website.
Inputs & Outputs (Personal Knowledge Management) is an in-
depth article with extensive, contextual links to additional
resources we did not create (and one we did).
VNLs can be arranged thematically as well. Create Better
Prospects (Part I and Part II) were published on Sept. 10 and Sept.
24, 2021. Earlier that summer we published a four-part series
(Courage & Perseverance) weekly over four weeks.
Each of those themes �t within the larger theme of our VNL
(Emergent Marketing).
We’re not sharing these examples to suggest they’re the ‘right’
way — instead, we’re showing the depth and breadth of what’s
possible within one approach to a VNL.
The opportunities are endless, and it’s up to you to craft a VNL
that works for your audience and for you, the creator.
However, you do NOT have to get that exactly right on day one. A
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 14 --

VNL evolves over time. Don’t try to determine what perfect looks
like before you take action. Start with something, then build from
there, layer by later, one VNL at a time.
Two more questions to answer are how often should I send Value
Newsletters and how long should each one be?
Again, there are no right answers to these questions.
How often is a combination of what’s best for your audience and
what you’re able to create consistently without hating the process.
Weekly, biweekly (aka fortnightly), monthly, or even randomly.
If you choose randomly, we recommend that you include an
approximate time frame — for example, “we send our newsletter
randomly, 7 — 10 times per year, rarely during the summer
months or during the month of December.”
That description would give a subscriber a sense of what to expect
without overcommitting to any speci�c details.
What matters most is that you do what you say you do — if you
say you send newsletters weekly on Fridays, send newsletters
weekly on Fridays. If you �nd that you’re making excuses once or
twice a month about why you missed a Friday, update what you’re
telling your audience to match reality. It’s that simple!
You’re in the driver’s seat with your Value Newsletter — act
accordingly.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 14 --

The length of each Value Newsletter is entirely up to you. Again,
that is a product of what the newsletter is supposed to achieve for
your audience, not some arbitrary minimum or maximum
number of words.
For reference, our Emergent Marketing Newsletters generally are
between 1,000 and 2,000 words, with many hovering around 1,500
words. That seems to feel right for us, but your results may vary.
Finally, there’s one last point to make about your Value
Newsletters that requires some nuance.
The decisions you make about the structure, content, and
frequency of your VNLs will combine to create a ‘cognitive load’
for you audience.
Let’s look at two extremes to better understand the point.
Version 1 is a monthly 1,000-word newsletter focused on one idea
with no links to additional resources. A typical reader could
consume and understand that email in 5-10 minutes. The
cognitive load on the reader is very low.
Version 2 is a daily 2,000-word newsletter focused on 2-3 ideas
with 10-20 links pointing to additional resources (articles, books,
podcasts) to deep dive into any or all of those idea. A typical reader
could consume that email in 15 — 20 minutes, with the potential
for 10x more (and beyond) that following links, reading, listening,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 14 --

etc. The cognitive load on the reader is very (very!) high.
These are extremes, of course, but they illustrate the point we’re
making.
We want our audience to consume our content, including our
Value Newsletters. We can accidentally make that dif�cult by
providing too much, too often, in ways that are too complex,
accidentally discouraging our audience from engaging.
There’s no formulaic method to �nd the sweet spot for the
cognitive load we create for our audience. And individuals will
have wildly different tolerances (which often will change
dynamically over time).
We recommend that you evaluate your Value Newsletters from
the perspective of your audience, and take time to evaluate your
newsletters within the context of what you’re asking your
audience to do to consume your content.
Again, there’s no right answer. However, start with a reasonably
accurate understanding of what you’re asking of your audience
and then adjust accordingly.
Your speci�c area of expertise, your relationship with your
audience, and the level of interest your audience has all are
factors. Sometimes those factors combine to make daily emails
effective, while other times those factors suggest monthly
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 14 --

newsletters are better.
The best way to determine this for yourself is to make an
educated guess, execute, and then evaluate results based on
audience engagement and feedback.
Other Newsletters
To round off this VNL Module, here are some of our favorite (and
varied) non-marketing newsletters to help expand your ideas of
what a good newsletter can look like.
We’re sharing these newsletters for the newsletter component, not
how each �ts into the business model of the sender. Each
newsletter is valuable to us for different reasons beyond just the
topics discussed.
In no particular order:
Tim Ferriss’ 5-Bullet Friday (varied topics). Tim shares �ve
thoughtful things he’s reading, watching, or has purchased, etc.
Kyle Westaway’s Weekend Brie�ng is around innovation and
society. Kyle is an attorney for startups, who, in his words, “works
at the intersection of pro�t and purpose.” This is a wonderfully
curated newsletter sent every Saturday, where Kyle added deep
context to every share.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 14 --

Dense Discovery is a weekly newsletter (each Tuesday) on tech,
design, ethics and sustainability. It’s a beautiful newsletter, always
thoughtful and valuable. We love the tools section each week. You
can access the archives here.
Arnold Schwarzenegger’s newsletter is so personal each monthly
issues feels like a memoir. They’re long and varied. You get a real
personal glimpse into the life of the superhero.
Morning Brew has become the 800-pound gorilla in the free
business newsletter space. It’s an example of a daily newsletter
done well.
The Prepared by Spencer Wright is a weekly newsletter about
research, analyses, and news from across the engineered,
manufactured, and built world. It’s free, with a premium version
for those wanting more interactions ($249/yr or $1,750/yr).
Stratechery by Ben Thompson is probably the preeminent
newsletter in the technology / strategy category, with subscribers
from all the big tech companies. Ben’s newsletter earns him well
over $3M/year. His Weekly Articles are free, while three Daily
Updates a week are for subscribers only. André has been a paid
subscriber since 2018.
Barking Up The Wrong Tree by Eric Barker is another favorite.
With over 300,000 subscribers, Eric brings science-based answers
and expert insight on how to be awesome at life.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 14 --

Hurry Slowly by Jocelyn K. Glei (archives).
With over 215,000 subscribers, The Good Trade resonates with
segment of people who care about sustainability, slow living, and
self-love. In their words, “We serve a highly-engaged readership of
people seeking to better care for themselves, their communities,
and the planet. ”
The Daily Good, their newsletter, is a 30-second morning
newsletter featuring soothing playlists, sustainable recipes, and
uplifting articles, and more.
Lastly, Yancey Strickler, cofounder of Kickstarter, explores deep
insights around technology, culture, and ideas around long-term
thinking in his monthly newsletter, The Ideaspace.
Please post your comments and questions below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 14 --
